items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.

items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: How many deputies sit in the Assemblee nationale?
items.why: Correct answer: 577. “348” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: When did France adopt euro notes and coins?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2002. “1999” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many senators sit in the French Senate?
items.why: Exact answer: 348. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
